当他还只有11岁的时候，这位被称为Dazzely的Roblox球员就以老式的方式享受游戏：他玩游戏。
早在2012年，大型在线游戏平台Roblox就为他提供了很多东西。他免费创造了他的第一个化身，设计了它的圆柱形头部，并给它厚实的身体穿上了衣服。然后，从Roblox.com上100多万种游戏的列表中进行选择，Dazzely让这个小个子男人参加了一场又一场的游戏：他跳过障碍赛道，给他扣上赛车，给他建造堡垒。使用名为Roblox Studio的自由软件，Dazzely甚至可以构建自己的游戏。这就是我们的想法：Roblox的创建者将这个平台设想为一个巨大的、不断变化的数字游乐场，孩子们可以在这里制作他们想要的任何东西-游戏、服装、建筑、风景。
Dazzely在成长过程中继续玩Roblox，平台也随着他的成熟而成熟。2013年，Roblox推出了一种将其虚拟货币Robux兑换回真实美元的方法。原始经济-开发商兜售太阳镜或限量版帽子等虚拟物品-演变成了更复杂的经济。年龄较大的孩子开始为彼此工作，扮演特殊的角色，如艺术家、建造者和游戏编剧。
今天的Roblox是其超过1.5亿月度活跃用户的产物。最初的沙盒现在看起来像是一个由青少年组成的好斗的网络国家，他们的人口与日本不相上下。而且这些美元是真实的：根据传感器塔的数据，仅在2020年上半年，玩家就通过移动设备向Roblox投入了超过4.9亿美元。Roblox预计，其用户开发人员-其中许多人是十几岁到二十出头的Roblox老兵-今年将从出售他们创作的访问权中赚取超过2.5亿美元。该公司的估值为40亿美元，当开发商套现时，每1美元约收取65美分。
现年19岁的达泽利的真名是迪伦·莱姆斯-奥尔森(Dylan Lemus-Olson)，在这个蓬勃发展的超级大都市里，他有一个独特的职业：他是YouTube上揭露丑闻的名人。他既是小报记者，也是互联网巨魔，他探访了Roblox的黑暗角落，并将自己的事迹上传到了视频平台，在那里他拥有超过5.3万名订户。事实证明，有很多影子供他探索。
对于Dazzely来说，2017年一个上学之夜的推送通知让他跌入了Roblox的红灯区-这是通过游戏玩家经常使用的在线聊天应用Discorde发出的直接信息。文本中包含一个指向Roblox游戏的超链接，简称为“公寓”(The Condo)。眼花缭乱地犹豫了一会儿，然后点击了。
“这他妈的是什么？”他回忆起自己的反应说。比赛的背景是几个房间和一个小厨房，后院有一个游泳池。它就像一个小公寓。这个游戏的名字也因此而得名。但引起他注意的不是楼层平面图。那是平房起居室里扭动的一群赤裸的Roblox化身。
他们是和所有Roblox游戏中一样的笨头笨脑、乐高风格的角色。除了这些化身炫耀着亵渎的夸张的解剖结构，他们头顶上的语言气泡是一团耸人听闻的咒骂和诽谤的云。言论自由也不是这些像素化的享乐主义者唯一在练习的东西。
他按下按钮开始录制他的屏幕。他靠在电脑的麦克风里，开始为他当时刚刚成立的YouTube频道提供幽默的解说。第二天早上，当Dazzely登录他的YouTube账户，看到他的视频已经获得了1000次点击量时，Roblox不再是他的孩子了。他找到了自己的使命。
对于外行来说，21世纪的游戏至少在一个方面类似于它的16位过去：玩家仍然将控制器和键盘混搭在一起，让角色在屏幕上跑来跑去。但是今天的游戏不仅仅是消磨时间。它们锚定了大量的社会生活珊瑚礁。粉丝们自己在游戏中一起玩，但他们也在邻近的网站附近学习-在Twitch上为彼此播放游戏，在YouTube上发布视频，通过不和谐的方式分享表情包。像“超级马里奥兄弟”(Super Mario Brothers)一样古老的游戏现在支持蓬勃发展的在线社区(谷歌“Kaizo Mario”)。但激发了一些最具活力的生态系统的游戏是那些高度开放的游戏，在那里用户可以自己玩--“我的世界”、“堡垒之夜”，尤其是“罗伯克斯”。
对于Roblox的联合创建者、长期担任首席执行官的大卫·巴祖基来说，这个庞大的社交世界是这款游戏最好的特色，也是即将到来的媒体虚拟世界的标志。他说，像Roblox这样的平台代表了“一种人类共同体验和自由的新类别”，一种融合了视频游戏和社交媒体的平台。“我们正处于这个独一无二的机会，可以成为它的牧羊人。”
这意味着要监督一个充满儿童的世界。虽然Roblox没有透露其用户的年龄，但它确实表示，他们中的大多数人都在18岁以下-而且至少有一半16岁及以下的美国儿童在该平台上。Comcore 2017年12月的一项研究发现，5岁至9岁的孩子玩Roblox的时间比在PC上做任何其他事情的时间都多；对于9岁至18岁的孩子来说，只有YouTube占用了他们更多的在线注意力。
在一个社会疏远的时代，这不一定是一件坏事。儿童发展专家越来越多地将这些多人空间视为孩子们关于社交规则、团队合作和自我调节的课程来源。网络游戏及其邻近的视频流和聊天网站也会产生归属感和同志情谊。坦普尔大学心理学研究员、“新童年”的作者乔丹·夏皮罗(Jordan Shapiro)说：“我们必须用理解操场游戏的方式来理解游戏。”
事实上，许多来到Roblox的孩子决心玩他们最喜欢的那种虚构的游戏：假装成人生活。模拟成人活动的游戏，比如照顾宠物和婴儿(“领养我！”)，找工作(“在披萨店工作”)，开车(“越狱”，“驾驶模拟器”)，总共吸引了数百亿的访问量。
“我们希望孩子们有机会自由玩耍，”巴祖基说。“Roblox允许他们聚在一起，制定自己的规则，一起去他们想去的任何地方。。。一起创造，一起建设，在很多情况下，一起学习。“。但正如Twitter、Facebook和YouTube的领导人所认识到的那样，在一个平台成长过程中保持对其的控制并非易事。而将于9月份迎来16岁生日的Roblox，还有一个额外的挑战，那就是陪伴一个为热爱软件修补的年轻用户量身定做的平台。
“Roblox是我学会黑客的地方，”奎恩·威尔顿(Quinn Wilton)说，她是一名安全研究员，童年时期玩这款游戏，现在软件安全公司Synopsys工作。威尔顿说，如果要在面对职业黑客和一群不守规矩的青少年之间做出选择，她每次都会选择前者。她说：“青少年为了自我、名誉和尊重而黑客攻击--他们有无穷无尽的时间。”
孩子们测试边界，青少年的欲望不能很好地适应服务条款。在Roblox上，这些精通数字的青少年正在快速成长-并将他们的头像和自己置于危险的境地。
当时是早上7点。5月的一个早晨，在东部，在一场名为“哈哈~~”的Roblox游戏中，只有几个玩家在由一个被称为Cons的团体创建的派对屋的棕榈树旁的游泳池里漫步。在现实生活中，这座房子可能是一个企业活动场所-你可以想象它通风的门厅里挤满了身穿斜纹棉布裤子和名牌的高管，护理着劣质红酒。Cons夫妇显然也设想了他们的客人也会认真对待成人事务，尽管是不同类型的。他们的窝点展示了一系列的性玩具。楼上的包间里只摆放了床。地下室是一个火炬点亮的性爱地牢。
新玩家在开始这款Roblox游戏时有一个选择：“男孩”、“女孩”或“其他”。选择前两个选项中的一个，立即脱去你的衣服，把你运到游泳池边；点击“其他”，你穿着衣服，在房间里放有性玩具。化身可以从超过25种不同的身体姿势和动作中进行选择，其中大多数都是模拟性行为。(男性也可以不协调地选择“俯卧撑”。)。啤酒和音箱也可以买到。
哈哈的房子在它存在的几个小时内最终会有超过600次的玩家参观，但目前它相对安静。在里面，唯一激动人心的是一个孤独的男性身影，他躺在家里的开放式厨房里，躺在早餐吧台上。从他的骨盆多边形附近的动作判断，他是在自娱自乐。在房子的其他地方，一名玩家向另一名玩家申请了一台私人游戏服务器-这样他们就可以进入派对屋的一个相同的、空的副本，单独进行他们的互动。
Roblox因其致力于保护用户而受到儿童安全倡导者的赞扬。它维护着严格的游戏规则，其中包括：没有网络欺凌和骚扰；没有歧视性、威胁性或过度暴力的行为；没有寻找约会对象或从事任何形式的性行为。13岁以下的玩家(你必须输入生日才能创建账户，尽管公司不会核实)被禁止接收来自其他用户的直接消息，除非父母将这些名字添加到朋友名单中。家长可以设置额外的控制，比如限制孩子玩经过策划、预先审查的游戏。
Roblox大力执行这些规则，使用软件过滤器审查游戏中的聊天内容，在人工智能的帮助下监视玩家的活动，并由1600多名人类内容审核员全天候应对问题。执法机构将该平台的多层次安全方法作为黄金标准。“Roblox在很大程度上是行业的领导者，”美国司法部儿童剥削和淫秽部门的负责人Steven Grocki说。
然而，多年来，该公司一直在悄悄地对自己的一些年轻用户发动一场技术影子战争。这些玩家利用Roblox来允许他们诅咒、上传露骨的内容，并以公司从未想过的方式操纵游戏。通过游戏生态系统的其他部分共享内容-最引人注目的是不和谐，允许用户通过文本、语音和视频进行一对一或分组交谈-他们利用了Roblox的执行机制在自己的平台上结束的事实。这是一个缺陷，可能会让Roblox用户受到的保护比他们的父母想象的要少得多。(Roblox表示，它“努力与其他聊天、社交媒体和用户生成的内容平台合作，举报不良演员和内容”，但要由这些平台采取“适当的行动”。)。
“这是一种对抗性的情况。。。(而且)我们知道坏演员是不会罢手的，“Roblox的一位高级安全代表说，他要求不具名。
Roblox和它的叛逆玩家之间的猫和老鼠的小规模冲突每天都会发生，而且是全天候的。哈哈的房子上线的同一天早上，罗伯克斯把它关闭了。但这几乎无关紧要：另外两个公寓游戏仍在进行，一个设在一个没有窗户的地堡里，上面贴着巴特·辛普森(Bart Simpson)和海绵宝宝(SpongeBob)的海报，另一个设在一个烟雾弥漫的洞穴夜总会里。当Roblox关闭这些网站时，还有其他网站如雨后春笋般涌现。Roblox上没有任何东西表明新的公寓游戏可能会在什么时候出现，或者它可能会被称为什么(“GAMEGAMEGAM”，“$的伤疤”)。然而，狂欢者发现了他们，他们的头像经常在游戏直播后的几秒钟内掉落。不久，一款流行的公寓游戏可能会有超过1000名玩家-所有人都会喝酒、蒸发、咒骂，而且通常都会表现出他们的块状怪癖。
他们的突然出现并不是魔术。游戏玩家最喜欢的网站星座为我们指明了方向。而且这些网站都有自己的内容标准。Roblox审查“公寓”这个词，但Reddit的游戏玩家群体可以很容易地讨论这类游戏。YouTube要求描述游戏最露骨时刻的视频带有年龄限制，但它允许任何人观看解释如何找到公寓游戏的视频。不一致是最有帮助的：FAST Company轻松定位了大约150个致力于显性Roblox内容的不一致组(也称为“服务器”)。
这些服务器--其中几台每台都有超过10,000名用户--是Roblox“坏演员”的流浪营地：游戏开拓者、内容旁观者和公寓创建者本身。他们利用不和谐的团体交往，吹嘘自己的功绩，分享新公寓游戏的链接，在游戏中相互聊天。不和谐并不禁止链接到Roblox性游戏，但表示所有成人内容必须降级到明确标记为18岁及以上的频道和服务器。该公司在一份声明中表示，如果发现用户谎报年龄，它将“立即采取行动”。(不一致不会验证用户注册时的年龄。)。
FAST Company采访了17名不和谐公寓服务器的成员，其中14人表示他们自己运营Roblox公寓游戏。所有人都说他们的年龄在13岁到17岁之间。有三人提供了他们的学生证照片，以证明他们是未成年人。(没有人同意让他们的父母接受采访。)。
在四周的时间里，这些玩家通过Discord的短信和语音聊天与Fast Company进行了交谈，他们称自己是长期使用Roblox的玩家，他们进入公寓是为了获得刺激、地位和金钱：他们说，侵入一个大平台并智胜Roblox委员是一项令人兴奋的挑战，出售游戏中的管理特权可能会带来丰厚的生意，这些特权允许玩家飞行和传送、操纵其他用户的化身，甚至把其他玩家踢出去。
查看这些共管公寓服务器的聊天日志--Fast Company提取并检查了其中31个服务器的聊天日志--一个游戏聊天流，如果用6号字体打印出来，将有超过91万页-描绘了一幅大规模在线游戏时代无人监督的青春期的图景。这些似乎是一只脚还植根于Roblox的孩子，Roblox是他们童年时的数字游戏空间，另一只脚延伸到了不和谐更明目张胆的成人世界。全小写的文字对话散发着典型青少年无聊的恶臭，新冠肺炎的避难所命令让这种状态变得更加糟糕。一位公寓业主说：“我宁愿在外面和朋友们一起闲逛。”
然而，这些没有细菌的虚拟空间几乎不安全，即使危险很难察觉。一些以共管公寓为主题的不和谐服务器将自己标榜为包容和友好。其他服务器充斥着种族诽谤和仇恨言论；一个致力于Roblox公寓游戏的不和谐服务器有一个非常活跃的用户，用户名为“WhiteSupremacist004”。
在公寓游戏的世界里，孩子们的行为像成年人一样，成年人可能会假扮成孩子，父母似乎完全缺席。与此同时，那些确实对正在发生的事情有优势的成年人-那些运行Roblox和不和谐的人-似乎没有能力完全关闭这些内容，也不愿公开分享他们所知道的一切。因此，在这些游戏和聊天中相互保护的重担往往落在青少年的肩上。
对于K来说，当他找不到玩家玩他的G级Roblox游戏时，他就开始了进入公寓的旅程。
这位16岁的少年奋斗了近三年-基本上是他整个青春期后的生活-才做出了一款热门游戏，但从未成功地从每天上传到Roblox的数万款新游戏中脱颖而出。因此，今年夏天，他付给了他在“不和谐”杂志上认识的一个人1000 Robux(约合10美元)，这个人给他发来了一个公寓游戏的完整代码，这样K.K就可以在每次Roblox的内容版主关闭它时复制它(只需做一些调整)。随着Roblox投资的进行，K认为这是一个稳操胜券的赌注。“我想，我也想在Roblox上发财，”他说，他指的是他遇到的玩家，他们拥有丰厚的Robux账户和大量的虚拟物品。然后他创建了自己的不和谐服务器并开始工作。
像大多数不和谐的公寓服务生一样，K的功能就像一个机场休息室，让会员们在等待下一场比赛时既忙又开心。在他命名为#Condo-Uploads的专用频道中，公寓游戏链接在他上传时到达，在Roblox关闭它们时离开。用户可以在#普通聊天中畅所欲言，或者跳上#NSFW频道，那里几乎不间断地播放淫秽照片和动画gif。(大型服务器有时会专门为Roblox黑社会中的其他黑市特产提供精品频道：游戏作弊、露骨的音乐黑客、自制的Roblox色情作品)。
在工作五天后接受Fast Company采访时，K说他觉得自己好像已经掌握了基本知识：他和几个朋友生成了一堆一次性的Roblox账户，启动了VPN来屏蔽他们的流量，并从他们的刻录机账户上传了一场又一场比赛。他说，他通过另一个Roblox账户销售虚拟t恤赚钱。当玩家穿着这些衬衫出现在他的公寓游戏中时，K会授予他们管理特权。他说，他设法赚了近3000个Robux，他把钱全部花在了Roblox的物品上。但一周后，K被赶出了公寓：Roblox已经识别了他的电脑设备ID，并“硬件屏蔽”了他。
其他公寓游戏运营商则更为老练。D说他今年13岁，当Fast Company在7月份与他交谈时，他已经经营了几个月的公寓。他知道自己的硬件ID，有一名志愿者在他不在的时候上传游戏并监视服务器馈送，甚至付钱让著名的Roblox黑客加入他的服务器-这是有影响力的营销的不和谐版本。D.还维护了一个独立的Discorde服务器，类似于公司内部网，他在那里发布员工指南并庆祝里程碑。他估计他每个月可以从他的游戏中赚取15万美元，如果他使用Roblox的货币兑换，大约450美元。(黑市上甚至还有更优惠的价格。)。那些知道自己在做什么的人可以在几年内继续上传公寓。Fast Company评估的两台最大的不和谐共管公寓服务器可以追溯到2018年。
接受采访的公寓运营商Fast Company中几乎没有人承认参观过他们自己的游戏。这可能会给他们带来“Oder”的名声，这是一个贬义的术语，意思是“网上约会的人”。一位房主说，他今年17岁，已经经营了一家公寓服务器两年多了，他确实愿意玩他的游戏，但对称他们为公寓嗤之以鼻。他说，他迎合了更成熟、更排外的用户群；他更喜欢“ERP”这个词，它代表“色情角色扮演”。
恕我直言，Maison ERP的客户，这些游戏一点也不色情。数字旋转是可笑的，如果不可能看不见的话：穿着丁字裤的Roblox化身在脱衣舞杆上扭动着；健壮的金发化身赤身裸体，除了COVID面具(在这款特定的游戏中，墙上写着“#stayathomeHub”)，还有足够多的原始动画扭曲，足以填满几卷“卡玛经”(如果乐高决定出版一个版本的话)。
从游戏中的评论判断，大多数Roblox玩家觉得Condo游戏很恶心，至少在他们第一次遇到它们的时候是这样。(“EEWWWW，”一名玩家在5月份来到一个公寓游戏时写道。)。但他们也发现它们是一种解放-数字版相当于父母不在城里的空房子。5月份，一名用户在一场公寓游戏中聊天说：“TBH我可能只是来这里骂人的。”“操他妈的性爱狗屎我来这里就是为了说脏话。”
令人担忧的正是这种结合--成人自由的感觉与图形的、不带感情的性行为的混合。两位研究儿童捕食者的专家查看了公寓游戏中的视频，他们宣称，他们为希望培养儿童防止性虐待的捕食者提供了教科书般的地形：“对于s。
.
note_en:
When he was still just 11 years old, the Roblox player known as Dazzely enjoyed games the old-fashioned way: He played them.
   Back in 2012, Roblox, the massive online gaming platform, had plenty to offer him. He created his first avatar for free, styled its cylindrical head and clothed its blocky body. Then, choosing from the list of over a million titles on Roblox.com, Dazzely placed the little man in one game after another: He jumped him through obstacle courses, buckled him into race cars, and built him forts. Using free software called Roblox Studio, Dazzely could even construct his own games. That was the idea: Roblox’s creators envisioned the platform as a vast and ever-changing digital playground where kids crafted whatever they desired—games, clothing, structures, landscapes.
 Dazzely continued playing Roblox as he grew up, and the platform matured with him. In 2013, Roblox introduced a way to convert its virtual currency, Robux, back into real dollars. A primitive economy—developers peddling virtual items like sunglasses or limited edition hats—evolved into a more complex one. Older kids began working for one another and performing specialized roles, as artists, builders, and game scripters.
  Roblox today is the product of its over 150 million monthly active users. What began as a sandbox now resembles a scrappy cybernation of teens and tweens whose population rivals that of Japan. And the dollars are real: Players poured more than $490 million into Roblox via mobile devices in the first half of 2020 alone, according to Sensor Tower data. Roblox projects that its user-developers—many of whom are Roblox veterans in their late teens and early twenties—will earn more than $250 million from selling access to their creations this year. The company, which is valued at $4 billion, takes roughly 65 cents of every U.S. dollar when developers cash out.
 Dazzely, who is now 19 and whose real name is Dylan Lemus-Olson, holds a unique occupation in this booming super-metropolis: He’s a muckraking YouTube personality. Part tabloid reporter, part internet troll, he pokes around Roblox’s dark corners and uploads his exploits to the video platform, where he has more than 53,000 subscribers. As it turns out, there are plenty of shadows for him to explore.
 For Dazzely, it was a push notification on a school night in 2017 that knocked him tumbling into Roblox’s red light district—a direct message via Discord, the online chat application that’s frequently used by gamers. The text contained a hyperlink to a Roblox game called, simply, “The Condo.” Dazzely hesitated briefly, then clicked.
  “What the fuck is this?” he says, recalling his reaction. The game’s setting was a couple of rooms and a kitchenette, a pool in the backyard. It resembled a small condominium. Hence the game’s name. But it wasn’t the floor plan that caught his attention. It was the writhing crowd of naked Roblox avatars in the bungalow’s living room.
 They were the same blocky-headed, Lego-esque characters as in all Roblox games. Except these avatars sported profanely exaggerated anatomies, and the speech bubbles above their heads were a lurid cloud of curse words and slurs. Freedom of speech wasn’t the only thing these pixelated hedonists were exercising, either.
  He hit a button to start recording his screen. He leaned into his computer’s microphone and began to provide humorous narration for his then-nascent YouTube channel. The following morning, when Dazzely logged into his YouTube account and saw that his video had already received 1,000 views, Roblox stopped being child’s play for him. He had found his calling.
 To the uninitiated, 21st-century gaming resembles its 16-bit past in one respect, at least: Players still mash controllers and keyboards to make characters run around the screen. But games today do more than kill time. They anchor teeming reefs of social life. Fans play together inside the games themselves, but they also school around adjacent sites—streaming their gameplay for one another on Twitch, posting videos on YouTube, sharing memes via Discord. Titles as ancient as   Super Mario Brothers  now support flourishing communities online (Google “Kaizo Mario”). But the games that inspire some of the most vibrant ecosystems are the massively open ones where users make their own fun—  Minecraft ,   Fortnite , and, especially, Roblox.
 For David Baszucki, Roblox’s cocreator and longtime CEO, this sprawling social world is the game’s best feature, and a sign of the media metaverse to come. Platforms like Roblox represent “a new category of human co-experience and freedom” he says, one that blends video games and social media. “We’re in this unique opportunity to be its shepherds.”
  That means overseeing a world that’s filled with children. Though Roblox doesn’t disclose the ages of its users, it does say that the majority of them are under 18—and that at least half of U.S. kids ages 16 and under are on the platform. A December 2017 study by Comscore found that kids between the ages of 5 and 9 spend more time playing Roblox than doing anything else online on PCs; for those between the ages of 9 and 18, only YouTube consumes more of their online attention.
 At a time of social distancing, that’s not necessarily a bad thing. Child development specialists increasingly see these multiplayer spaces as a source of lessons for kids about social rules, teamwork, and self-regulation. Online games and their adjacent video-streaming and chat sites also engender a sense of belonging and camaraderie. “We have to understand games the same way we understand playground play,” says Jordan Shapiro, a psychology researcher at Temple University and author of   The New Childhood .
 Indeed, many kids arrive on Roblox determined to play their favorite kind of make-believe: pretending at adult life. Games simulating grownup activities, such as caring for pets and babies (“Adopt Me!”), getting jobs (“Working at a Pizza Place”), and driving cars (“Jailbreak,” “Driving Simulator”), collectively rack up tens of billions of visits.
  “We want kids to have an opportunity for free play,” Baszucki says. “Roblox allows them to get together, make their own rules, go together wherever they might want . . . and create together, build together, and in many cases, learn together.” But as the leaders of Twitter, Facebook, and YouTube have learned, maintaining control of a platform as it grows up isn’t easy. And Roblox, which turns 16 in September, has the added challenge of chaperoning a platform tailor-made for young users who love tinkering with software.
 “Roblox is where I learned to hack,” says Quinn Wilton, a security researcher who spent her childhood playing the game and now works at the software security firm Synopsys. Given the choice between facing off against professional hackers or a mob of unruly teens, Wilton says she’d pick the former every time. “A teenager is hacking for ego, fame, respect—and they have endless amounts of time,” she says.
  Kids test boundaries, and teenage desires don’t fit neatly into terms of service.  On Roblox, these digitally savvy adolescents are growing up fast—and putting their avatars and themselves in dangerous positions.
  It was 7 a.m. eastern on a May morning, and in a Roblox game titled “Haha~~;)” only a few  players  meandered around the palm tree-lined pool at a party house created by a group known as The Cons. In real life, this house might have functioned as a corporate event space—one could imagine its airy foyer filled with executives in chinos and name tags, nursing bad red wine. The Cons clearly envisioned their guests getting down to adult business as well, albeit of a different sort. Their den showcased an array of sex toys. The private rooms upstairs were furnished only with beds. The basement was a torch-lit sex dungeon.
 New players started this Roblox game with a choice: “Boy,” “Girl,” or “other.” Selecting one of the first two options immediately stripped your clothes and transported you poolside; clicking “other” placed you, clothed, in the room with sex toys. Avatars could choose among more than 25 different body positions and movements—most of them simulating sex acts. (Males could also, incongruously, choose “pushups.”) Beer and boom boxes were available too.
 The Haha house would eventually be visited by players more than 600 times in its few hours of existence, but for now it was relatively quiet. Inside, the only soul stirring was a solitary male figure in the home’s open-concept kitchen, recumbent on the breakfast bar. Judging by the motions proximate to his pelvis polygons, he was pleasuring himself. Elsewhere in the house, a player requested a private  game  server with another—so they could enter an identical, empty copy of the party house and carry on their interactions alone.
 Roblox has been praised by child safety advocates for its dedication to safeguarding users. It maintains strict in-game rules, among them: no cyberbullying and harassment; no discriminatory, threatening, or overly violent behavior; and no looking for dates or engaging in sexual behavior of any kind. Players under 13 (you must enter a birthdate to create an account, though the company does not verify it) are prohibited from receiving direct messages from other users unless parents add those names to a friends list. Parents can set up additional controls, such as limiting the child to playing curated, pre-vetted games.
  Roblox enforces these rules vigorously, censoring in-game chats with software filters, watching player activity with the help of AI, and responding to problems with a battalion of more than 1,600 human content moderators, 24/7. Law enforcement agencies have held up the platform’s multilayered approach to security as a gold standard. “Roblox is very much an industry leader,” says Steven Grocki, chief of the Child Exploitation and Obscenity Section at the U.S. Department of Justice.
 Yet for years, the company has quietly waged a technological shadow war against some of its own young users. These players have exploited Roblox to allow them to curse, upload explicit content, and manipulate games in ways the company never intended. And by sharing content via other parts of the gaming ecosystem—most notably Discord, which allows users to talk to one another via text, voice, and video, either one-on-one or in groups—they exploit the fact that Roblox’s enforcement mechanisms end at its own platform. It’s a shortcoming that can leave Roblox users far less protected than their parents might think. (Roblox says it works “diligently with other chat, social media, and User Generated Content platforms to report bad actors and content,” though it’s up to these platforms to take “appropriate action.”)
 “This is an adversarial kind of situation . . . [and] we know that the bad actors are not going to stop,” says a top Roblox safety representative, who requested that his name be withheld.
 The cat-and-mouse skirmishes between Roblox and its rebellious players take place daily and around the clock. The same morning that the Haha house went live, Roblox shut it down. But that hardly mattered: Two other condo games were still running, one set in a windowless bunker with posters of Bart Simpson and SpongeBob, another in a cavernous, smoke-filled nightclub. When Roblox shut those down, still others sprouted up. Nothing on Roblox indicates when a new condo game might appear, or what it might be called (“GAMEGAMEGAM,” “SCAR OF$”). Yet the revelers find them, their avatars often dropping in mere seconds after games go live. Before long, a popular condo game may have upwards of 1,000 players—all drinking, vaping, swearing, and generally getting their blocky freak on.
 Their sudden appearance isn’t magic. The constellation of gamers’ favorite websites points the way. And these sites each have their own content standards. Roblox censors the word “condo,” but Reddit’s gamer groups can easily discuss these kinds of games. YouTube requires videos that depict the games’ most explicit moments to carry an age restriction, but it allows anyone to watch videos that explain how to find condo games. Discord is most helpful:   Fast Company  easily located some 150 Discord groups (also called “servers”) that are devoted to explicit Roblox content.
 These servers—a couple of which have more than 10,000 users each—are home to roving camps of Roblox’s “bad actors”: the game-exploiters, the content-bypassers, and the condo creators themselves. They use Discord groups to mingle, boast about their exploits, share links to new condo games, and chat amongst themselves while in the game. Discord doesn’t prohibit linking to Roblox sex games, but says that all adult content must be relegated to channels and servers explicitly marked for ages 18 and older. In a statement, the company  said that it “takes immediate action” if it finds out that users have lied about their age. (Discord doesn’t verify users’ ages at sign-up.)
  Fast Company  interviewed 17 members of Discord  condo servers, including 14 who said they operated Roblox condo games themselves. All said they were between the ages of 13 and 17. Three provided photos of their school IDs to prove that they are underage. (None agreed to make their parents available for interviews.)
 These gamers, who spoke with   Fast Company  via Discord’s text and voice chat over a period of four weeks, describe themselves as longtime Roblox players who got into condos for the thrills, status, and money involved: Hacking a big platform and outwitting the Roblox commissars is an exciting challenge, they say, and there can be tidy business in selling in-game administrative privileges, which allow a player to fly and teleport, manipulate other users’ avatars, or even kick out other players.
 Reviewing the chat-logs of these condo servers—  Fast Company  extracted and examined the logs from 31 of them, a stream of gamerchat that would run over 910,000 pages if printed out in six-point font—painted a picture of unsupervised adolescence in the age of massive online games. These appear to be kids with one foot still planted in Roblox, the digital playspace of their childhood, and the other extended to Discord’s more brazenly adult world. The all-lowercase text conversations reek of classic teenage boredom, a state made all the worse by COVID-19’s shelter-in-place orders. “I’d rather be outside hanging with my friends or something,” one condo owner said.
  Yet these germ-free virtual spaces are hardly safe, even when the danger is difficult to discern. Some condo-themed Discord servers advertise themselves as inclusive and friendly. Other servers are riddled with racial slurs and hate speech; one Discord server devoted to Roblox condo games had a very active user with the username “WhiteSupremacist004.”
 The world of condo games is  a place where kids act like adults, adults may be posing as kids, and parents seem to be completely absent. Meanwhile the grown-ups who do have a vantage point into what’s going on—those running Roblox and Discord—seem incapable of fully shutting down this content and unwilling to publicly share all that they know. As a result, the burden of protecting one another in these games and chats often falls on the teens’ shoulders.
  For K., the journey into condos began when he couldn’t find players for his G-rated Roblox games.
 The 16-year-old struggled for nearly three years—his entire postpubescent life, basically—to make a hit game, but never managed to stand out from the tens of thousands of new games that are uploaded to Roblox every day. So this summer, he paid 1,000 Robux, or around $10, to a guy he’d met on Discord, who sent him the complete code for a condo game, which would allow K. to replicate it (with a few tweaks) each time Roblox’s content moderators shut it down. As Roblox investments went, K. felt it was a sure bet. “I’m like, I want to be rich on Roblox, too,” he said, referencing players he’d met with fat Robux accounts and virtual items galore. Then he created his own Discord server and got to work.
 Like most Discord condo servers, K.’s functioned like an airport lounge, keeping members occupied and happy while they awaited the next game. In a dedicated channel that he named #condo-uploads, condo game links arrived as he uploaded them and departed as Roblox shut them down. Users could shoot the breeze in the #general chat, or hop on the #NSFW channels, which housed a near-constant stream of obscene photos and animated gifs. (Larger servers sometimes dedicate boutique channels to other black-market specialties in the Roblox underworld: game cheats, explicit music hacks, homemade Roblox porn).
 Speaking to   Fast Company  after five days on the job, K. said he felt as though he had the basics down: He and a couple friends generated a bunch of throwaway Roblox accounts, fired up a VPN to mask their traffic, and uploaded game after game from their burner accounts. He made money, he said, by selling virtual t-shirts through a different Roblox account. When players showed up in his condo game wearing one of these shirts, K. would grant them administrative privileges. He managed to make nearly 3,000 Robux, he says, money which he spent entirely on Roblox items. But a week later, K. was knocked out of the condo racket: Roblox had identified his computer’s device ID and “hardware-blocked” him.
 Other condo-game operators are more sophisticated. D., who says he’s 13 years old, had been operating condos for months when   Fast Company  spoke to him in July. He knew enough to hide his hardware ID, had a volunteer staff who uploaded games and kept an eye on the server feeds when he wasn’t available, and even paid notable Roblox hackers to join his server—a Discord version of influencer marketing. D. also maintained a separate Discord server, akin to a company intranet, where he posted staff guidelines and celebrated milestones. He estimated that he was making some 150,000 Robux a month from his games,  or about $450 if he used Roblox’s currency exchange. (There are even better rates on the black market.)  Those who know what they’re doing can keep uploading condos for years. Two of the largest Discord condo servers that   Fast Company  evaluated had been active dating back to 2018.
 Few of the condo operators   Fast Company  spoke with admit to visiting their own games. That might get them a reputation as an “ODer,” a derogatory term meaning “online dater.”  One owner, who says he is 17 and has been running a condo server for over two years, did cop to playing his games, but sniffs at calling them condos. He says he caters to a more mature, exclusive userbase; he prefers the term “ERP,” which stands for “erotic role-play.”
  With all respect to the clientele of maison ERP, there’s nothing remotely erotic about these games. The digital gyrations are ridiculous, if impossible to unsee: thong-clad Roblox avatars twerking on stripper poles; beefy blond avatars buck naked except for COVID masks (written on the walls in this particular game: “#stayathomehub”), and enough crudely animated contortions to fill several volumes of the   Kama Sutra  (should Lego ever decide to publish an edition).
 Judging by the in-game comments, most Roblox players find Condo games gross, at least when they first encounter them. (“EEEEWWWW,” wrote one player, upon arriving in a condo game in May.) But they also find them liberating—the digital equivalent of an empty house where the parents are out of town. “tbh ill probably only come here to swear,” chatted one user in a condo game in May. “fuck dat sex shit im here for the bad words.”
 It’s exactly this combination—the perception of adult freedoms mixed with graphic, emotionless sex—that’s worrisome. Two experts on child predators who reviewed footage from condo games declared that they presented textbook terrain for predators looking to groom children for sexual abuse: “For s
......
title: 调查揭示了Roblox与“公寓游戏”的斗争，“公寓游戏”是一种数字性派对，在这种游戏中，孩子们表现得像成年人，成年人可能会伪装成孩子
title_en: Investigation sheds light on Roblox's battle against “condo games”, digital sex parties where kids act like adults and adults may be posing as kids

今年6月，Reddit开始了一场巨大的运动，从其网站上删除令人憎恨的内容，屏蔽从事仇恨言论和骚扰的社区。在今天的最新进展中，该公司表示，它已经禁止了近7000个Subreddits(包括惯犯)，导致发布仇恨内容的用户减少了18%。
Reddit用户u/worstnerd是该公司安全团队的成员，他写道：“虽然我希望这个数字是100%，但我对这个进展感到鼓舞。”
“虽然我希望这个数字是百分之百，但我对这一进展感到鼓舞。”
这一进展是Reddit发生翻天覆地变化的一部分，此前该网站引入了明确禁止仇恨言论的新政策，并承诺严格执行这些政策。Reddit长期以来一直因容忍种族主义和歧视女性的内容而受到批评，该网站的高管为自己的立场辩护，称其维护言论自由。在过去，这意味着容忍分享偷来的裸照或家庭暴力视频的社区，或者宣扬白人民族主义等意识形态的社区。
作为新立场的一部分，Reddit表示将更密切地研究仇恨言论在网站上的传播。在6月份开始的“禁令浪潮”之前，该公司表示，每天大约有4万条潜在的令人憎恨的内容被分享，约占所有内容的0.2%。这些帖子、评论和消息累计浏览量约为647万次，约占每日总浏览量的0.16%。(浏览量百分比较小，因为审核机器人会在任何人看到内容之前将其删除。)。该公司没有说明这些数字是如何因禁令浪潮而发生变化的。
该公司还表示，该网站上所有令人憎恨的内容中有一半(48%)是针对一个人的种族或国籍的。紧随其后的是他们的阶级或政治背景(16%)，他们的性取向(12%)，他们的性别(10%)，或他们的宗教(6%)，而1%的仇恨内容针对能力，7%的人目标不明确。
Reddit表示，这些数据是初步的，未来将提供更详细的数据。但它强调，收集此类信息在规模上极具挑战性。
“有时仇恨可以非常公开，有时可以更微妙。U/worstnerd写道，在其他情况下，历史上被边缘化的群体可能会重新使用语言，并以他们可以接受、但其他人不能接受的方式使用。“此外，人们在如何刻薄对待彼此的问题上有着怪异的创造力。他们不断发展自己的语言，让外人(和模特)难以理解。
note_en:
In June this year, Reddit began a huge campaign to  remove hateful content from its site, blocking communities that engaged in hate speech and harassment. In an  update on its progress today, the company says it’s banned nearly 7,000 subreddits (including repeat offenders) resulting in an 18 percent drop in users posting hateful content.
 “While I would love that number to be 100 percent, I’m encouraged by the progress,” writes Reddit user  u/worstnerd, a member of the company’s Safety team.
 “While I would love that number to be 100 percent, I’m encouraged by the progress.”
 The progress is part of a sea change at Reddit after the site introduced new policies that explicitly ban hate speech and promised to enforce them rigorously. Reddit has long been criticized for tolerating racist and misogynistic content, with the site’s executives defending its position as upholding free speech. In the past, that has meant tolerating communities that shared stolen nude photos, or videos of domestic violence, or promoted ideologies like white nationalism.
 As part of its new stance, Reddit says it will be studying the spread of hate speech on the site more closely. Prior to the “ban wave” that began in June, the company says approximately 40,000 potentially hateful pieces of content were shared each day, making up around 0.2 percent of all content. These posts, comments, and messages accumulated some 6.47 million views or around 0.16 percent of total daily views. (The view percentage is smaller because moderation bots remove some content before anyone sees it.) The company did not say how these figures had changed as a result of the ban wave.
   The company also says that half (48 percent) of all hateful content on the site was targeting a person’s ethnicity or nationality. That was followed by their class or political affiliation (16 percent), their sexuality (12 percent), their gender (10 percent), or their religion (6 percent), while 1 percent of hate content targeted ability and 7 percent had an unclear target.
 Reddit says these figures are preliminary and that it will be offering more detailed data in the future. But it stressed that collecting such information is extremely challenging at scale.
 “Sometimes hate can be very overt, other times it can be more subtle. In other circumstances, historically marginalized groups may reclaim language and use it in a way that is acceptable for them, but unacceptable for others to use,” writes u/worstnerd. “Additionally, people are weirdly creative about how to be mean to each other. They evolve their language to make it challenging for outsiders (and models) to understand.”
title: Reddit表示，自6月份以来，它已经禁止了近7000个Subreddits，导致发布仇恨内容的用户减少了18%
title_en: Reddit says it has banned nearly 7,000 subreddits since June, resulting in an 18% drop in users posting hateful content

谷歌今天为其Pixel Buds添加了许多新功能，同时也解决了一些早期买家经历过的切断/断开连接的问题。最新的固件更新应该会减少“通话过程中中断的情况”，并具有“当一个耳塞或两个耳塞断开连接时更好的自动恢复”。一种新的抄写模式被设计成将长篇演讲直接翻译到你的耳朵里，如果你正在听一场演讲或演讲，其中一个人说的是你听不懂的语言，这是非常理想的。Pixel Buds一直支持翻译功能，但这种转录模式是为单向翻译设计的，而不是来回对话，而且它是一种使用Buds进行翻译的更离散的方式。它最初将提供法语、德语、意大利语和西班牙语到英语的翻译。
除了新的转录模式，谷歌还增加了一些音频选项。新的低音增强模式增强了您从Pixel Buds听到的低音。这与更好的共享检测功能很好地结合在一起，现在Pixel Buds用户可以与某人共享一个耳塞，并对每个耳塞进行单独的音量控制。
谷歌今天还将推出一项新的Pixel Buds实验功能。“注意提醒”旨在提醒Pixel Buds用户在听音乐时周围发生的重要事件。如果附近有婴儿哭喊，或者急救车辆开着警笛经过，Pixel Buds会自动暂时降低音量，这样你就能听到周围发生的事情。
如果你是一个经常把你的Pixel Buds放错地方的人，谷歌也在改进找到它们的能力。虽然你已经可以按响耳塞来找到它们，但现在你还可以在地图上看到它们最后的已知位置。谷歌助手也对Pixel Buds进行了一些改进。你现在可以问“我的耳机还有多少电池续航时间？”助理会接电话。你也可以通过谷歌助手启用或禁用Pixel Buds的触控。
今天，所有这些新功能都将在谷歌的Pixel Buds上推出。
note_en:
Google is adding a number of new features to its Pixel Buds today — and also addressing the cutout / disconnect issues that some early buyers have experienced. The latest firmware update should decrease “instances of cut-outs during calls” and have “better auto-recovery when one or both earbuds lose connection.” A new  transcribe mode is designed to translate long speeches directly into your ears, which is ideal for if you’re listening to a lecture or speech where a single person is speaking in a language you don’t understand. The Pixel Buds have always supported a translation feature, but this transcribe mode is designed for one-way translation instead of conversations back and forth, and it’s a more discrete way to translate using the Buds. It will be available initially for French, German, Italian, and Spanish translation into English.
 Alongside the new transcribe mode, Google is also adding some more audio options. A new bass boost mode enhances the bass you hear from the Pixel Buds. This pairs well with a better sharing detection feature, which now allows Pixel Buds users to share a single earbud with someone and have individual volume control over each earbud.
   Google is also launching a new Pixel Buds experimental feature today.  Attention Alerts are designed to alert Pixel Buds users of important events happening around them while they’re listening to music. If a baby cries nearby or an emergency vehicle passes by with its siren on, the Pixel Buds will automatically lower the volume temporarily so you can hear what’s happening around you.
 If you’re someone who frequently misplaces your Pixel Buds, Google is also improving the ability to find them. While you can already ring the earbuds to find them, you can also now see their last known location on a map. Google Assistant is also getting some improvements on the Pixel Buds. You can now ask “how much battery life do I have left on my earbuds?” and Assistant will answer. You can also enable or disable the Pixel Buds’ touch controls through Google Assistant.
 All of these new features are rolling out to Google’s Pixel Buds today.
title: 谷歌的Pixel Buds获得了一个新的转录模式，可以翻译长篇演讲，注意提醒用户发生在他们周围的重要事件，等等
title_en: Google's Pixel Buds get a new transcribe mode to translate long speeches, Attention Alerts to alert users of important events happening around them, and more
